title: The Bookshop That Vanished
text_zh: 城南有一家书店，我确信自己去过，但没有任何人能证实它的存在。那是一个秋天的傍晚，我在一条从未走过的巷子里迷了路，然后看到了一扇半开的木门，门楣上挂着一块褪色的匾额，写着「不期而遇书屋」。店内的灯光昏黄，书架从地面一直延伸到天花板，每一层都塞满了旧书。空气中弥漫着陈年纸张特有的微甜的霉味——那种味道让人想到地下室、阁楼和被遗忘的时间。店主是一个头发花白的老人，戴着一副圆框眼镜，正坐在柜台后面读一本已经翻得很旧的书。他抬头看了我一眼，没有说话，只是微微点了点头——那个动作的含义似乎是「随便看看吧」，又似乎是「我知道你会来的」。我在那里待了大约一个小时，翻了很多书，最后买了一本薄薄的诗集。付钱的时候我注意到，老人用的不是计算器，而是一把老式的算盘。他算得很慢，珠子碰撞发出清脆的声响，在安静的书店里格外分明。第二天我想再去的时候，那条巷子还在，但书店消失了——那扇门变成了一面完整的砖墙，仿佛从来不曾有过任何开口。我至今保留着那本诗集，但版权页上印着的出版社名字，我在任何数据库里都查不到。
text_pinyin: Chéng nán yǒu yī jiā shūdiàn, wǒ quèxìn zìjǐ qù guò, dàn méiyǒu rènhé rén néng zhèngshí tā de cúnzài. Nà shì yī gè qiūtiān de bàngwǎn, wǒ zài yī tiáo cóng wèi zǒu guò de xiàngzi lǐ mí le lù, ránhòu kàn dào le yī shàn bàn kāi de mù mén, ménméi shàng guà zhe yī kuài tuì sè de biǎn'é, xiě zhe「Bùqī'éryù Shūwū」. Diàn nèi de dēngguāng hūnhuáng, shūjià cóng dìmiàn yīzhí yánshēn dào tiānhuābǎn, měi yī céng dōu sāi mǎn le jiù shū. Kōngqì zhōng mímàn zhe chénnián zhǐzhāng tèyǒu de wēi tián de méi wèi——nà zhǒng wèidào ràng rén xiǎng dào dìxiàshì, gélóu hé bèi yíwàng de shíjiān. Diànzhǔ shì yī gè tóufa huābái de lǎorén, dài zhe yī fù yuán kuàng yǎnjìng, zhèng zuò zài guìtái hòumiàn dú yī běn yǐjīng fān de hěn jiù de shū. Tā táitóu kàn le wǒ yī yǎn, méiyǒu shuōhuà, zhǐ shì wēiwēi diǎn le diǎn tóu——nàge dòngzuò de hányì sìhū shì「suíbiàn kànkan ba」, yòu sìhū shì「wǒ zhīdào nǐ huì lái de」. Wǒ zài nàlǐ dāi le dàyuē yī gè xiǎoshí, fān le hěn duō shū, zuìhòu mǎi le yī běn báobáo de shījí. Fù qián de shíhou wǒ zhùyì dào, lǎorén yòng de bú shì jìsuànqì, ér shì yī bǎ lǎoshì de suànpán. Tā suàn de hěn màn, zhūzi pèngzhuàng fāchū qīngcuì de shēngxiǎng, zài ānjìng de shūdiàn lǐ géwài fēnmíng. Dì èr tiān wǒ xiǎng zài qù de shíhou, nà tiáo xiàngzi hái zài, dàn shūdiàn xiāoshī le——nà shàn mén biànchéng le yī miàn wánzhěng de zhuān qiáng, fǎngfú cóng lái bù céng yǒu guò rènhé kāikǒu. Wǒ zhìjīn bǎoliú zhe nà běn shījí, dàn bǎnquán yè shàng yìn zhe de chūbǎnshè míngzì, wǒ zài rènhé shùjùkù lǐ dōu chá bú dào.
text_en: In the south of the city there was a bookshop I am certain I visited, yet no one can confirm its existence. It was an autumn evening; I had gotten lost in an alley I'd never walked before, when I noticed a half-open wooden door with a faded signboard above it reading 'Serendipity Books.' The interior lighting was dim amber, bookshelves stretched from floor to ceiling, each shelf packed with old books. The air was thick with the slightly sweet mustiness unique to aged paper—a smell evoking basements, attics, and forgotten time. The owner was a white-haired old man wearing round spectacles, sitting behind the counter reading a well-worn book. He glanced up at me, said nothing, and merely nodded slightly—a gesture that seemed to mean 'browse as you like,' or perhaps 'I knew you would come.' I stayed about an hour, leafed through many books, and finally bought a slim poetry collection. When paying I noticed the old man used not a calculator but an old-fashioned abacus. He calculated slowly, the beads clicking crisply, uncommonly distinct in the quiet shop. The next day when I tried to return, the alley was still there, but the bookshop had vanished—the door had become a solid brick wall, as if no opening had ever existed. I still have that poetry collection, but the publisher's name printed on the copyright page cannot be found in any database.

title: The Pianist in the Rain
text_zh: 每到雨天，小区花园里就会传来钢琴声。这件事本身并不奇怪——奇怪的是，花园里没有钢琴，也没有任何能够发出钢琴声的设备。我第一次注意到它是在一个梅雨季节的午后。雨淅淅沥沥地下着，我站在窗边看雨，突然听到了一段旋律——是德彪西的《雨中花园》。琴声不是从哪栋楼里传出来的，而是像从雨水本身中流淌出来的，仿佛每一滴雨落在地面、树叶和金属扶手上时，都被一只看不见的手编排成了音符。我下楼去寻找声音的来源。花园里空无一人，只有雨在下。但那段旋律依然清晰——我甚至能分辨出弹奏者在某些段落使用了踏板，因为音符之间有一种特殊的延绵感。我问了小区里的很多邻居。有些人说他们也听到过，但各人描述的曲目却不一样——有人听到的是肖邦，有人听到的是舒伯特，有人听到的是一首完全不认识的曲子。唯一的共同点是：只在雨天出现，只在花园附近能听到，而且每个人都觉得那个旋律恰好是自己最喜欢的那首。我后来查了小区的历史档案，发现这里在建成住宅之前是一所音乐学校，据说有一位年轻的钢琴教师在一个雨夜里突发心脏病去世了。但这或许只是巧合。也或许雨水本身就懂得音乐——毕竟，水滴击打不同材质的表面时产生的频率组合，在某些条件下确实可能形成类似旋律的声学现象。但这个解释太过理性，让人觉得遗憾——我更愿意相信花园里住着一个弹钢琴的幽灵，他只在雨天演奏，而且总是弹每个听众最喜欢的曲子。
text_pinyin: Měi dào yǔtiān, xiǎoqū huāyuán lǐ jiù huì chuán lái gāngqín shēng. Zhè jiàn shì běnshēn bìng bù qíguài——qíguài de shì, huāyuán lǐ méiyǒu gāngqín, yě méiyǒu rènhé nénggòu fāchū gāngqín shēng de shèbèi. Wǒ dì yī cì zhùyì dào tā shì zài yī gè méiyǔ jìjié de wǔhòu. Yǔ xīxī-lìlì de xià zhe, wǒ zhàn zài chuāng biān kàn yǔ, tūrán tīng dào le yī duàn xuánlǜ——shì Débīxī de《Yǔ Zhōng Huāyuán》. Qín shēng bú shì cóng nǎ dòng lóu lǐ chuán chūlái de, ér shì xiàng cóng yǔshuǐ běnshēn zhōng liútǎng chūlái de, fǎngfú měi yī dī yǔ luò zài dìmiàn, shùyè hé jīnshǔ fúshǒu shàng shí, dōu bèi yī zhī kàn bú jiàn de shǒu biānpái chéng le yīnfú. Wǒ xià lóu qù xúnzhǎo shēngyīn de láiyuán. Huāyuán lǐ kōng wú yī rén, zhǐ yǒu yǔ zài xià. Dàn nà duàn xuánlǜ yīrán qīngxī——wǒ shènzhì néng fēnbiàn chū tánzòu zhě zài mǒuxiē duànluò shǐyòng le tàbǎn, yīnwèi yīnfú zhī jiān yǒu yī zhǒng tèshū de yánmián gǎn. Wǒ wèn le xiǎoqū lǐ de hěn duō línjū. Yǒuxiē rén shuō tāmen yě tīng dào guò, dàn gè rén miáoshù de qǔmù què bù yīyàng——yǒu rén tīng dào de shì Xiāobāng, yǒu rén tīng dào de shì Shūbótè, yǒu rén tīng dào de shì yī shǒu wánquán bú rènshi de qǔzi. Wéiyī de gòngtóng diǎn shì: zhǐ zài yǔtiān chūxiàn, zhǐ zài huāyuán fùjìn néng tīng dào, érqiě měi gè rén dōu juéde nàge xuánlǜ qiàhǎo shì zìjǐ zuì xǐhuan de nà shǒu. Wǒ hòulái chá le xiǎoqū de lìshǐ dàng'àn, fāxiàn zhèlǐ zài jiànchéng zhùzhái zhīqián shì yī suǒ yīnyuè xuéxiào, jùshuō yǒu yī wèi niánqīng de gāngqín jiàoshī zài yī gè yǔ yè lǐ tūfā xīnzàng bìng qùshì le. Dàn zhè huòxǔ zhǐ shì qiǎohé. Yě huòxǔ yǔshuǐ běnshēn jiù dǒngde yīnyuè——bìjìng, shuǐdī jīdǎ bùtóng cáizhì de biǎomiàn shí chǎnshēng de pínlǜ zǔhé, zài mǒuxiē tiáojiàn xià quèshí kěnéng xíngchéng lèisì xuánlǜ de shēngxué xiànxiàng. Dàn zhège jiěshì tài guò lǐxìng, ràng rén juéde yíhàn——wǒ gèng yuànyì xiāngxìn huāyuán lǐ zhù zhe yī gè tán gāngqín de yōulíng, tā zhǐ zài yǔtiān yǎnzòu, érqiě zǒng shì tán měi gè tīngzhòng zuì xǐhuan de qǔzi.
text_en: Every rainy day, piano music drifts through the neighborhood garden. This itself is unremarkable—what is strange is that the garden has no piano, nor any equipment capable of producing piano sound. I first noticed it on a plum-rain-season afternoon. The rain pattered gently; I stood by the window watching it when I suddenly heard a melody—Debussy's 'Jardins sous la pluie.' The piano sound did not come from any building but seemed to flow from the rain itself, as if every drop striking the ground, leaves, and metal railings was arranged into notes by an invisible hand. I went downstairs to find the source. The garden was deserted; only the rain was falling. Yet the melody remained clear—I could even tell the performer used the sustain pedal in certain passages, because there was a special lingering quality between notes. I asked many neighbors. Some said they had heard it too, but each described a different piece—one heard Chopin, another Schubert, another a completely unfamiliar composition. The only common thread: it appeared only on rainy days, could only be heard near the garden, and every person felt the melody happened to be their favorite piece. I later searched the neighborhood's historical records and discovered that before the residential complex was built, this had been a music school, and reportedly a young piano teacher had died of a sudden heart attack on a rainy night. But this is perhaps merely coincidence. Or perhaps rain itself understands music—after all, the frequency combinations produced when water drops strike surfaces of different materials can, under certain conditions, genuinely form acoustic phenomena resembling melody. But this explanation is too rational, leaving one with a sense of regret—I would rather believe a piano-playing ghost lives in the garden, performing only in the rain, always playing each listener's favorite piece.

title: The Map That Drew Itself
text_zh: 我在祖父的遗物中发现了一张手绘地图。地图画在一张泛黄的宣纸上，用毛笔勾勒出了一个我完全不认识的地方——蜿蜒的河流、密集的等高线、几个用小圆圈标注的村庄，以及一条用虚线标出的、似乎通向某个终点的小路。地图的右下角标注着一个日期：一九四七年十一月初八。我尝试用各种方法辨认这个地方的位置。我对照了当地的县志，查阅了民国时期的测绘资料，甚至请教了一位退休的地理学教授——但没有任何一处真实的地形与这张地图吻合。地图上标注的那些村庄名字，在任何历史记录中都不存在。起初我以为这是祖父凭想象画的，但地图的精确程度令人震惊。等高线的间距完全符合专业测绘规范，河流的弯曲度遵循了自然地貌中的侵蚀力学原理，甚至连北斗标志的朝向都严格按照了制图学的标准。一个虚构的地方不需要如此精确。最令我困惑的是那条虚线小路。它从地图左上角的一个村庄出发，蜿蜒穿过山谷和溪流，最终在地图的正中央戛然而止——虚线在那里断开了，旁边用很小的字写着两个字：「未至」。祖父一生都住在同一个小镇上，从未远行。这张地图描绘的到底是一个他去过但我找不到的地方，还是一个他想去但终究没有到达的地方？「未至」二字，是一个事实的陈述，还是一个未完成的愿望？
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài zǔfù de yíwù zhōng fāxiàn le yī zhāng shǒuhuì dìtú. Dìtú huà zài yī zhāng fànhuáng de xuānzhǐ shàng, yòng máobǐ gōulè chū le yī gè wǒ wánquán bú rènshi de dìfāng——wānyán de héliú, mìjí de děnggāo xiàn, jǐ gè yòng xiǎo yuánquān biāozhù de cūnzhuāng, yǐjí yī tiáo yòng xūxiàn biāo chū de, sìhū tōng xiàng mǒu gè zhōngdiǎn de xiǎo lù. Dìtú de yòu xià jiǎo biāozhù zhe yī gè rìqī: yī jiǔ sì qī nián shíyī yuè chū bā. Wǒ chángshì yòng gè zhǒng fāngfǎ biànrèn zhège dìfāng de wèizhì. Wǒ duìzhào le dāngdì de xiànzhì, cháyuè le Mínguó shíqī de cèhuì zīliào, shènzhì qǐngjiào le yī wèi tuìxiū de dìlǐ xué jiàoshòu——dàn méiyǒu rènhé yī chù zhēnshí de dìxíng yǔ zhè zhāng dìtú wěnhé. Dìtú shàng biāozhù de nàxiē cūnzhuāng míngzì, zài rènhé lìshǐ jìlù zhōng dōu bù cúnzài. Qǐchū wǒ yǐwéi zhè shì zǔfù píng xiǎngxiàng huà de, dàn dìtú de jīngquè chéngdù lìng rén zhènjīng. Děnggāo xiàn de jiānjù wánquán fúhé zhuānyè cèhuì guīfàn, héliú de wānqū dù zūnxún le zìrán dìmào zhōng de qīnshí lìxué yuánlǐ, shènzhì lián Běidǒu biāozhì de cháoxiàng dōu yángé ànzhào le zhìtú xué de biāozhǔn. Yī gè xūgòu de dìfāng bù xūyào rúcǐ jīngquè. Zuì lìng wǒ kùnhuò de shì nà tiáo xūxiàn xiǎo lù. Tā cóng dìtú zuǒ shàng jiǎo de yī gè cūnzhuāng chūfā, wānyán chuānguò shāngǔ hé xīliú, zuìzhōng zài dìtú de zhèng zhōngyāng jiárán ér zhǐ——xūxiàn zài nàlǐ duàn kāi le, pángbiān yòng hěn xiǎo de zì xiě zhe liǎng gè zì:「Wèi zhì」. Zǔfù yīshēng dōu zhù zài tóng yī gè xiǎo zhèn shàng, cóng wèi yuǎn xíng. Zhè zhāng dìtú miáohuì de dàodǐ shì yī gè tā qù guò dàn wǒ zhǎo bú dào de dìfāng, háishi yī gè tā xiǎng qù dàn zhōngjiù méiyǒu dàodá de dìfāng?「Wèi zhì」èr zì, shì yī gè shìshí de chénshù, háishi yī gè wèi wánchéng de yuànwàng?
text_en: Among my grandfather's possessions I found a hand-drawn map. It was rendered on yellowed rice paper, brush-inked outlines of a place I completely did not recognize—winding rivers, dense contour lines, several villages marked with small circles, and a path marked in dotted lines that seemed to lead toward some destination. In the lower right corner was a date: November 8th, 1947. I tried every method to identify this location. I cross-referenced local county gazetteers, consulted Republican-era survey materials, even sought the expertise of a retired geography professor—but no real terrain matched this map. The village names marked on it exist in no historical record. At first I assumed grandfather had drawn it from imagination, but the map's precision was astonishing. The contour line intervals perfectly met professional surveying standards, the river curvature followed the erosion mechanics of natural landforms, and even the compass rose orientation strictly adhered to cartographic conventions. An imaginary place does not require such precision. Most puzzling was the dotted path. Starting from a village in the upper left corner, it wound through valleys and streams, only to stop abruptly in the map's exact center—the dotted line broke off there, and beside it, in very small characters, were written two words: 'Not yet arrived.' My grandfather lived his entire life in the same small town and never traveled far. Does this map depict a place he visited that I cannot find, or a place he wished to go but ultimately never reached? Are the words 'not yet arrived' a statement of fact, or an unfulfilled wish?

title: The Clock That Gained a Minute
text_zh: 我家客厅里挂着一座老式挂钟，是曾祖父从上海带回来的。那座钟已经走了将近一百年，几乎从未出过差错——唯一的例外是每年的同一天，七月十四日，它会在午夜十二点的时候多走整整一分钟。也就是说，在那一天，从十一点五十九分到零点零一分之间，挂钟上实际经过了六十一分钟。这个异常是我父亲最先发现的。他是一个对时间极其敏感的人——那种会为了一列火车晚了三十秒而不安的人。有一年他偶然在七月十四日的午夜注意到挂钟和手表之间出现了一分钟的误差，从此之后他年年都会守在钟前观察。他做了详细的记录，持续了二十三年——无一例外，每年七月十四日午夜，挂钟都会多走一分钟。我曾经请一位钟表修理师检查过这座钟。他把整个机芯拆开，每一个齿轮、每一根弹簧都仔细检查过，然后告诉我一切正常，没有任何机械原因能解释这个现象。他甚至开玩笑说：「也许这座钟记住了某个特别的夜晚，每年都想让那个夜晚多延续一会儿。」我后来查了家族的旧记录，发现曾祖父去世的日期正是七月十四日。他在上海的一家医院里去世，死亡时间记录为午夜零点整。也许那座钟并没有出错——也许它只是在用自己的方式，每年为它的第一个主人多保留一分钟的时间。一分钟很短，短到在日常生活中几乎可以忽略不计。但对于一座忠诚的钟来说，那一分钟是它能给出的最长的告别。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā kètīng lǐ guà zhe yī zuò lǎoshì guàzhōng, shì zēngzǔfù cóng Shànghǎi dài huílái de. Nà zuò zhōng yǐjīng zǒu le jiāngjìn yī bǎi nián, jīhū cóng wèi chū guò chācuò——wéiyī de lìwài shì měi nián de tóng yī tiān, qī yuè shísì rì, tā huì zài wǔyè shí'èr diǎn de shíhou duō zǒu zhěngzhěng yī fēnzhōng. Yě jiù shì shuō, zài nà yī tiān, cóng shíyī diǎn wǔshíjiǔ fēn dào líng diǎn líng yī fēn zhī jiān, guàzhōng shàng shíjì jīngguò le liùshí yī fēnzhōng. Zhège yìcháng shì wǒ fùqīn zuì xiān fāxiàn de. Tā shì yī gè duì shíjiān jíqí mǐngǎn de rén——nà zhǒng huì wèile yī liè huǒchē wǎn le sānshí miǎo ér bù'ān de rén. Yǒu yī nián tā ǒurán zài qī yuè shísì rì de wǔyè zhùyì dào guàzhōng hé shǒubiǎo zhī jiān chūxiàn le yī fēnzhōng de wùchā, cóngcǐ zhīhòu tā nián nián dōu huì shǒu zài zhōng qián guānchá. Tā zuò le xiángxì de jìlù, chíxù le èrshísān nián——wú yī lìwài, měi nián qī yuè shísì rì wǔyè, guàzhōng dōu huì duō zǒu yī fēnzhōng. Wǒ céngjīng qǐng yī wèi zhōngbiǎo xiūlǐ shī jiǎnchá guò zhè zuò zhōng. Tā bǎ zhěnggè jīxīn chāi kāi, měi yī gè chǐlún, měi yī gēn tánhuáng dōu zǐxì jiǎnchá guò, ránhòu gàosu wǒ yīqiè zhèngcháng, méiyǒu rènhé jīxiè yuányīn néng jiěshì zhège xiànxiàng. Tā shènzhì kāi wánxiào shuō:「Yěxǔ zhè zuò zhōng jì zhù le mǒu gè tèbié de yèwǎn, měi nián dōu xiǎng ràng nàge yèwǎn duō yánxù yīhuìr.」Wǒ hòulái chá le jiāzú de jiù jìlù, fāxiàn zēngzǔfù qùshì de rìqī zhèng shì qī yuè shísì rì. Tā zài Shànghǎi de yī jiā yīyuàn lǐ qùshì, sǐwáng shíjiān jìlù wéi wǔyè líng diǎn zhěng. Yěxǔ nà zuò zhōng bìng méiyǒu chū cuò——yěxǔ tā zhǐ shì zài yòng zìjǐ de fāngshì, měi nián wèi tā de dì yī gè zhǔrén duō bǎoliú yī fēnzhōng de shíjiān. Yī fēnzhōng hěn duǎn, duǎn dào zài rìcháng shēnghuó zhōng jīhū kěyǐ hūlüè bù jì. Dàn duìyú yī zuò zhōngchéng de zhōng lái shuō, nà yī fēnzhōng shì tā néng gěi chū de zuì cháng de gàobié.
text_en: In our living room hangs an old wall clock, brought back from Shanghai by my great-grandfather. That clock has run for nearly a hundred years and has almost never been wrong—with one exception: every year on the same day, July 14th, it gains exactly one minute at midnight. That is, on that day, between 11:59 PM and 12:01 AM, the clock actually registers sixty-one minutes. My father first noticed this anomaly. He was a person extremely sensitive to time—the kind who would be unsettled by a train arriving thirty seconds late. One year he happened to notice at midnight on July 14th that the wall clock and his wristwatch had developed a one-minute discrepancy. From then on, he stood vigil before the clock every year. He kept detailed records for twenty-three years—without exception, every July 14th at midnight, the clock gained one minute. I once had a clockmaker inspect it. He disassembled the entire movement, examined every gear and spring carefully, then told me everything was normal; no mechanical cause could explain the phenomenon. He even joked: 'Perhaps this clock remembers a particular night and every year wants that night to last a little longer.' I later checked old family records and discovered that my great-grandfather's date of death was precisely July 14th. He died in a hospital in Shanghai; the recorded time of death was exactly midnight. Perhaps the clock has not been wrong—perhaps it has merely been using its own method to preserve one extra minute of time each year for its first owner. One minute is very short, so short it can be almost ignored in daily life. But for a loyal clock, that minute is the longest farewell it can offer.

title: The Garden at the Wrong Address
text_zh: 我收到过一封寄给「城西路十七号花园」的信，但我住在城西路十七号，这里从来没有过花园——只有一栋普通的六层居民楼和一小片水泥停车场。信封上没有寄信人的地址，邮戳模糊得无法辨认日期。我犹豫了一下，还是拆开了。信的内容用工整的繁体字写成，措辞古雅，像是民国时期的文人笔调。信中说：「春日渐暖，园中玉兰已开。去年你赠我的那盆茉莉，经过一冬的休眠，今日竟也抽出了新芽。甚是欢喜。近来事务繁杂，久未通信，心中歉疚。上次你来信提到想在东墙下种一片紫藤，我以为甚好——紫藤花落时如一场紫色的雨，必定美极。待你下次来访时，我们一同商议布局。盼复。」信的落款只有一个字——「瑾」。我在城西路住了八年，从未听说过这里有过花园。但我请教了一位研究本地史的退休教师。他查阅了民国时期的地籍图册后告诉我，城西路十七号在上世纪三十年代确实是一座私家花园，主人姓林，花园里种满了玉兰和茉莉。而「瑾」这个名字，他在一份1940年的户籍档案中找到了——林瑾，女，生于1912年，职业一栏写的是「教员」。之后的记录就断了，仿佛她在1940年之后就从所有档案中消失了。那封信是寄给一个八十多年前就不存在的花园的，但它今天才到达。邮局的中转延误最多几周——八十年，这不是延误，这是时间本身出了差错。
text_pinyin: Wǒ shōu dào guò yī fēng jì gěi「Chéngxī Lù shíqī hào huāyuán」de xìn, dàn wǒ zhù zài Chéngxī Lù shíqī hào, zhèlǐ cóng lái méiyǒu guò huāyuán——zhǐ yǒu yī dòng pǔtōng de liù céng jūmín lóu hé yī xiǎo piàn shuǐní tíngchē chǎng. Xìnfēng shàng méiyǒu jì xìn rén de dìzhǐ, yóuchuō móhu de wúfǎ biànrèn rìqī. Wǒ yóuyù le yīxià, háishi chāi kāi le. Xìn de nèiróng yòng gōngzhěng de fántǐ zì xiě chéng, cuòcí gǔyǎ, xiàng shì Mínguó shíqī de wénrén bǐdiào. Xìn zhōng shuō:「Chūnrì jiàn nuǎn, yuán zhōng yùlán yǐ kāi. Qùnián nǐ zèng wǒ de nà pén mòlì, jīngguò yī dōng de xiūmián, jīnrì jìng yě chōu chū le xīn yá. Shèn shì huānxǐ. Jìnlái shìwù fánzá, jiǔ wèi tōngxìn, xīn zhōng qiànjiù. Shàng cì nǐ lái xìn tí dào xiǎng zài dōng qiáng xià zhòng yī piàn zǐténg, wǒ yǐwéi shèn hǎo——zǐténg huā luò shí rú yī chǎng zǐsè de yǔ, bìdìng měi jí. Dài nǐ xià cì lái fǎng shí, wǒmen yītóng shāngyì bùjú. Pàn fù.」Xìn de luòkuǎn zhǐ yǒu yī gè zì——「Jǐn」. Wǒ zài Chéngxī Lù zhù le bā nián, cóng wèi tīngshuō guò zhèlǐ yǒu guò huāyuán. Dàn wǒ qǐngjiào le yī wèi yánjiū běndì shǐ de tuìxiū jiàoshī. Tā cháyuè le Mínguó shíqī de dìjí túcè hòu gàosu wǒ, Chéngxī Lù shíqī hào zài shàng shìjì sānshí niándài quèshí shì yī zuò sījiā huāyuán, zhǔrén xìng Lín, huāyuán lǐ zhǒng mǎn le yùlán hé mòlì. Ér「Jǐn」zhège míngzì, tā zài yī fèn yī jiǔ sì líng nián de hùjí dàng'àn zhōng zhǎo dào le——Lín Jǐn, nǚ, shēng yú yī jiǔ yī èr nián, zhíyè yī lán xiě de shì「jiàoyuán」. Zhīhòu de jìlù jiù duàn le, fǎngfú tā zài yī jiǔ sì líng nián zhīhòu jiù cóng suǒyǒu dàng'àn zhōng xiāoshī le. Nà fēng xìn shì jì gěi yī gè bāshí duō nián qián jiù bù cúnzài de huāyuán de, dàn tā jīntiān cái dàodá. Yóujú de zhōngzhuǎn yánwù zuì duō jǐ zhōu——bāshí nián, zhè bú shì yánwù, zhè shì shíjiān běnshēn chū le chācuò.
text_en: I once received a letter addressed to 'No. 17 Chengxi Road Garden,' but I live at No. 17 Chengxi Road, which has never had a garden—just an ordinary six-story apartment building and a small concrete parking lot. The envelope bore no return address, and the postmark was too blurred to read. I hesitated, then opened it. The letter was written in neat traditional characters with elegant, classical phrasing, like the style of a Republican-era intellectual. It read: 'Spring grows warmer; the magnolias in the garden have bloomed. The jasmine you gifted me last year, after a winter's dormancy, has surprisingly put out new shoots today. I am so pleased. Lately affairs have been busy and I have long neglected correspondence; I feel guilty about this. In your last letter you mentioned wanting to plant wisteria along the east wall—I think this excellent. When wisteria petals fall they are like a purple rain; it will be exceedingly beautiful. When you next visit, we shall discuss the layout together. Awaiting your reply.' The letter was signed with a single character—'Jin.' I have lived on Chengxi Road for eight years and never heard of a garden here. But I consulted a retired teacher who researches local history. After checking Republican-era cadastral atlases, he told me that No. 17 Chengxi Road in the 1930s was indeed a private garden, owned by someone surnamed Lin, planted full of magnolias and jasmine. And the name 'Jin' he found in a 1940 household registration file—Lin Jin, female, born 1912, profession listed as 'teacher.' After that, the records simply stopped, as if she vanished from all archives after 1940. The letter was addressed to a garden that ceased to exist over eighty years ago, yet it arrived today. Postal transit delays run a few weeks at most—eighty years is not a delay; it is time itself making an error.

title: The Photograph Before the Camera
text_zh: 去年整理老宅时，我在阁楼的一个铁皮箱子里找到了一张照片。照片已经泛黄卷曲，但画面依然清晰——那是我家老宅的正门，门前的石榴树、台阶上的裂缝、甚至门楣上那副对联的字迹，都和我记忆中一模一样。照片背面用铅笔写着一行字：「摄于甲午年秋」。甲午年——按照最近的甲午年推算，那是一八九四年。然而，摄影术在十九世纪末虽然已经传入中国，但仅限于通商口岸的大城市。我们家所在的这个内陆小镇，在那个年代不可能有相机。更何况，这不是一张正式的人像照——那个时代的照片几乎都是在照相馆里拍摄的肖像。这张照片拍的是一栋普通的房子，构图随意，角度微微倾斜，更像是某个人路过时随手拍下的——但一八九四年不存在「随手拍」这种行为。我把照片拿给几位摄影史的专家看。他们对照片的纸张和显影工艺进行了分析，结论是：这张照片使用的是二十世纪二十年代才出现的银盐明胶工艺，绝不可能是一八九四年冲洗的。也就是说，要么背面的日期写错了，要么有人在一九二〇年代用当时的技术冲印了一张拍摄于一八九四年的底片。但后者意味着一个更大的谜团：那个年代的底片是玻璃板，不可能保存三十年还能冲印。而且，谁会在三十年后冲印一张普通房子的照片？除非那栋房子对他来说一点都不普通。
text_pinyin: Qùnián zhěnglǐ lǎo zhái shí, wǒ zài gélóu de yī gè tiěpí xiāngzi lǐ zhǎo dào le yī zhāng zhàopiàn. Zhàopiàn yǐjīng fànhuáng juǎnqū, dàn huàmiàn yīrán qīngxī——nà shì wǒ jiā lǎo zhái de zhèng mén, mén qián de shíliú shù, táijiē shàng de lièfèng, shènzhì ménméi shàng nà fù duìlián de zìjì, dōu hé wǒ jìyì zhōng yīmó-yīyàng. Zhàopiàn bèimiàn yòng qiānbǐ xiě zhe yī háng zì:「Shè yú jiǎwǔ nián qiū」. Jiǎwǔ nián——ànzhào zuìjìn de jiǎwǔ nián tuīsuàn, nà shì yī bā jiǔ sì nián. Rán'ér, shèyǐng shù zài shíjiǔ shìjì mò suīrán yǐjīng chuán rù Zhōngguó, dàn jǐn xiàn yú tōngshāng kǒu'àn de dà chéngshì. Wǒmen jiā suǒzài de zhège nèilù xiǎo zhèn, zài nàge niándài bù kěnéng yǒu xiàngjī. Gèng hékuàng, zhè bú shì yī zhāng zhèngshì de rénxiàng zhào——nàge shídài de zhàopiàn jīhū dōu shì zài zhàoxiàngguǎn lǐ pāishè de xiàoxiàng. Zhè zhāng zhàopiàn pāi de shì yī dòng pǔtōng de fángzi, gòutú suíyì, jiǎodù wēiwēi qīngxié, gèng xiàng shì mǒu gè rén lùguò shí suíshǒu pāi xià de——dàn yī bā jiǔ sì nián bù cúnzài「suíshǒu pāi」zhè zhǒng xíngwéi. Wǒ bǎ zhàopiàn ná gěi jǐ wèi shèyǐng shǐ de zhuānjiā kàn. Tāmen duì zhàopiàn de zhǐzhāng hé xiǎnyǐng gōngyì jìnxíng le fēnxī, jiélùn shì: zhè zhāng zhàopiàn shǐyòng de shì èrshí shìjì èrshí niándài cái chūxiàn de yín yán míngjīao gōngyì, jué bù kěnéng shì yī bā jiǔ sì nián chōngxǐ de. Yě jiù shì shuō, yàome bèimiàn de rìqī xiě cuò le, yàome yǒu rén zài yī jiǔ èr líng niándài yòng dāngshí de jìshù chōngyìn le yī zhāng pāishè yú yī bā jiǔ sì nián de dǐpiàn. Dàn hòu zhě yìwèi zhe yī gè gèng dà de mítuán: nàge niándài de dǐpiàn shì bōli bǎn, bù kěnéng bǎocún sānshí nián hái néng chōngyìn. Érqiě, shuí huì zài sānshí nián hòu chōngyìn yī zhāng pǔtōng fángzi de zhàopiàn? Chúfēi nà dòng fángzi duì tā lái shuō yīdiǎn dōu bù pǔtōng.
text_en: Last year while sorting through the old family house, I found a photograph in a tin box in the attic. The photo had yellowed and curled but the image remained clear—it showed the front entrance of our family's old house, the pomegranate tree by the door, the cracks on the steps, even the calligraphy of the couplet above the doorframe, all exactly as I remembered. On the back, in pencil, was written: 'Taken in the autumn of the Jiawu year.' The Jiawu year—calculating the most recent one, that would be 1894. Yet though photography had reached China by the late nineteenth century, it was limited to major cities at treaty ports. Our inland small town could not possibly have had a camera in that era. Moreover, this was not a formal portrait—photographs of that period were almost all studio portraits. This photo captured an ordinary house, casually composed, slightly tilted in angle, more like a snapshot taken by someone passing by—but 'snapshots' did not exist as a behavior in 1894. I showed the photograph to several photography historians. They analyzed the paper and developing process and concluded: the photo used silver gelatin technology that did not appear until the 1920s; it could not possibly have been developed in 1894. This means either the date on the back is wrong, or someone in the 1920s used contemporary technology to print a negative taken in 1894. But the latter implies an even greater mystery: negatives of that era were glass plates that could not possibly survive thirty years in printable condition. And who would print a photo of an ordinary house thirty years later? Unless that house was not ordinary to them at all.

title: The Voice in the Well
text_zh: 村子里那口老井已经枯了很多年，但偶尔经过时，如果你把耳朵贴近井口，会听到一种极其微弱的声音——不是回声，也不是风灌进去的呼啸，而是一种类似于低语的、断断续续的人声。你听不清它在说什么，但你能分辨出那是人的声音，而且似乎在诉说着什么。村里的老人们对这口井有各种各样的说法。有人说井底住着一条龙，那是龙在梦中的呓语。有人说那是地下暗河的水声，通过井壁的共振产生了类似人声的效果。还有人说，多年前有一个年轻女人在这口井边洗衣服时不慎坠入，虽然被救了上来，但她的声音永远留在了井底。最后这个说法是最令人不安的——不是因为它恐怖，而是因为它暗示了一种可能性：一个人的声音可以脱离身体独立存在，像一段录音被刻进了石壁里，在特定的条件下被播放出来。我有一次鼓起勇气，在深夜独自来到井边。月光把井口照成了一个银白色的圆，底下是看不见底的黑暗。我俯身侧耳倾听——那个声音比白天更清晰了，我甚至听出了两个音节，听起来像是一个名字。但当我试图再靠近一些时，一阵突然的冷风从井底升起，带着一股潮湿的泥土气味。我本能地后退了一步，那个声音也随即消失了——好像它只在你不太在意的时候出现，一旦你认真去听，它就羞怯地沉默了。
text_pinyin: Cūnzi lǐ nà kǒu lǎo jǐng yǐjīng kū le hěn duō nián, dàn ǒu'ěr jīngguò shí, rúguǒ nǐ bǎ ěrduo tiē jìn jǐngkǒu, huì tīng dào yī zhǒng jíqí wēiruò de shēngyīn——bú shì huíshēng, yě bú shì fēng guàn jìnqù de hūxiào, ér shì yī zhǒng lèisì yú dīyǔ de, duànduàn-xùxù de rén shēng. Nǐ tīng bù qīng tā zài shuō shénme, dàn nǐ néng fēnbiàn chū nà shì rén de shēngyīn, érqiě sìhū zài sùshuō zhe shénme. Cūn lǐ de lǎorénmen duì zhè kǒu jǐng yǒu gè zhǒng gè yàng de shuōfǎ. Yǒu rén shuō jǐng dǐ zhù zhe yī tiáo lóng, nà shì lóng zài mèng zhōng de yìyǔ. Yǒu rén shuō nà shì dìxià àn hé de shuǐ shēng, tōngguò jǐng bì de gòngzhèn chǎnshēng le lèisì rén shēng de xiàoguǒ. Hái yǒu rén shuō, duō nián qián yǒu yī gè niánqīng nǚrén zài zhè kǒu jǐng biān xǐ yīfu shí bú shèn zhuì rù, suīrán bèi jiù le shànglái, dàn tā de shēngyīn yǒngyuǎn liú zài le jǐng dǐ. Zuìhòu zhège shuōfǎ shì zuì lìng rén bù'ān de——bú shì yīnwèi tā kǒngbù, ér shì yīnwèi tā ànshì le yī zhǒng kěnéng xìng: yī gè rén de shēngyīn kěyǐ tuōlí shēntǐ dúlì cúnzài, xiàng yī duàn lùyīn bèi kè jìn le shí bì lǐ, zài tèdìng de tiáojiàn xià bèi bōfàng chūlái. Wǒ yǒu yī cì gǔ qǐ yǒngqì, zài shēnyè dúzì lái dào jǐng biān. Yuèguāng bǎ jǐngkǒu zhào chéng le yī gè yín bái sè de yuán, dǐxia shì kàn bú jiàn dǐ de hēi'àn. Wǒ fǔ shēn cè ěr qīngtīng——nàge shēngyīn bǐ báitiān gèng qīngxī le, wǒ shènzhì tīng chū le liǎng gè yīnjié, tīng qǐlái xiàng shì yī gè míngzì. Dàn dāng wǒ shìtú zài kào jìn yīxiē shí, yī zhèn tūrán de lěngfēng cóng jǐng dǐ shēng qǐ, dài zhe yī gǔ cháoshī de nítǔ qìwèi. Wǒ běnnéng de hòutuì le yī bù, nàge shēngyīn yě suíjí xiāoshī le——hǎoxiàng tā zhǐ zài nǐ bú tài zàiyì de shíhou chūxiàn, yīdàn nǐ rènzhēn qù tīng, tā jiù xiūqiè de chénmò le.
text_en: The old well in the village has been dry for many years, but if you occasionally pass by and press your ear to the rim, you hear an extremely faint sound—not an echo, not the whistle of wind rushing in, but something like a whisper, a halting, intermittent human voice. You cannot make out what it says, but you can tell it is a human voice, seemingly recounting something. The village elders have various explanations for this well. Some say a dragon lives at the bottom and those are its sleep-murmurs. Others say it is the sound of an underground river, its resonance through the well walls producing a human-voice-like effect. Still others say that years ago a young woman fell into this well while washing clothes at its edge; though she was rescued, her voice remained forever at the bottom. This last explanation is the most unsettling—not because it is frightening, but because it implies a possibility: a person's voice can exist independently of the body, like a recording etched into stone walls, played back under certain conditions. I once summoned the courage to visit the well alone at midnight. Moonlight turned the well mouth into a silver-white circle; beneath was bottomless darkness. I leaned over and listened—the voice was clearer than during the day, and I even made out two syllables that sounded like a name. But when I tried to lean closer, a sudden cold wind rose from the well bottom, carrying the damp smell of earth. I instinctively stepped back, and the voice vanished at once—as if it only appeared when you weren't paying close attention, and the moment you listened earnestly, it shyly fell silent.

title: The Passenger Who Arrived Early
text_zh: 火车站的工作人员都记得那个旅客——一个穿着过时的中山装、提着棕色皮箱的中年男人。他在一个普通的周二下午走进了候车室，径直走到三号检票口前面排队。问题是，三号检票口当天没有列车。工作人员提醒他检查车票，他掏出一张纸质车票——不是现在的电子打印票，而是那种老式的硬纸板车票，上面用红色油墨印着出发站和目的站，还有一排用打孔机打出的小洞。车票上的日期是下周二。他提前了整整一周。工作人员告诉他票上的日期是下周的，请他下周再来。男人低头看了看车票，脸上露出了困惑的表情。他说：「不可能，我妻子让我今天来的。她从来不会记错日期。」然后他拎起箱子离开了。没有人注意到他是什么时候走出候车室的——就好像他是突然从视线中蒸发的。一周后的同一天，三号检票口确实有一班列车，但没有任何旅客持有那种老式硬纸板车票——事实上，那种车票在十五年前就已经停止使用了。工作人员把这件事当成了一个古怪的小插曲，很快就忘了。直到三个月后，车站进行安全监控录像的例行备份时，技术人员发现了一件怪事——那个周二下午的监控录像中，候车室里看不到那个穿中山装的男人。摄像头覆盖了候车室的每一个角落，录像显示在他应该出现的时间段里，他排队的那个位置始终是空的。
text_pinyin: Huǒchēzhàn de gōngzuò rényuán dōu jìde nàge lǚkè——yī gè chuān zhe guòshí de zhōngshānzhuāng, tí zhe zōngsè píxiāng de zhōngnián nánrén. Tā zài yī gè pǔtōng de zhōu'èr xiàwǔ zǒu jìn le hòuchēshì, jìngzhí zǒu dào sān hào jiǎnpiào kǒu qiánmiàn páiduì. Wèntí shì, sān hào jiǎnpiào kǒu dāngtiān méiyǒu lièchē. Gōngzuò rényuán tíxǐng tā jiǎnchá chēpiào, tā tāo chū yī zhāng zhǐzhì chēpiào——bú shì xiànzài de diànzǐ dǎyìn piào, ér shì nà zhǒng lǎoshì de yìng zhǐbǎn chēpiào, shàngmiàn yòng hóngsè yóumò yìn zhe chūfā zhàn hé mùdì zhàn, hái yǒu yī pái yòng dǎkǒng jī dǎ chū de xiǎo dòng. Chēpiào shàng de rìqī shì xià zhōu'èr. Tā tíqián le zhěngzhěng yī zhōu. Gōngzuò rényuán gàosu tā piào shàng de rìqī shì xià zhōu de, qǐng tā xià zhōu zài lái. Nánrén dītóu kàn le kàn chēpiào, liǎn shàng lùchū le kùnhuò de biǎoqíng. Tā shuō:「Bù kěnéng, wǒ qīzi ràng wǒ jīntiān lái de. Tā cóng lái bú huì jì cuò rìqī.」Ránhòu tā līn qǐ xiāngzi líkāi le. Méiyǒu rén zhùyì dào tā shì shénme shíhou zǒu chū hòuchēshì de——jiù hǎoxiàng tā shì tūrán cóng shìxiàn zhōng zhēngfā de. Yī zhōu hòu de tóng yī tiān, sān hào jiǎnpiào kǒu quèshí yǒu yī bān lièchē, dàn méiyǒu rènhé lǚkè chí yǒu nà zhǒng lǎoshì yìng zhǐbǎn chēpiào——shìshí shàng, nà zhǒng chēpiào zài shíwǔ nián qián jiù yǐjīng tíngzhǐ shǐyòng le. Gōngzuò rényuán bǎ zhè jiàn shì dàng chéng le yī gè gǔguài de xiǎo chāqǔ, hěn kuài jiù wàng le. Zhídào sān gè yuè hòu, chēzhàn jìnxíng ānquán jiānkòng lùxiàng de lìxíng bèifèn shí, jìshù rényuán fāxiàn le yī jiàn guài shì——nàge zhōu'èr xiàwǔ de jiānkòng lùxiàng zhōng, hòuchēshì lǐ kàn bú dào nàge chuān zhōngshānzhuāng de nánrén. Shèxiàngtóu fùgài le hòuchēshì de měi yī gè jiǎoluò, lùxiàng xiǎnshì zài tā yīnggāi chūxiàn de shíjiān duàn lǐ, tā páiduì de nàge wèizhì shǐzhōng shì kōng de.
text_en: The station staff all remembered that passenger—a middle-aged man in an outdated Mao suit, carrying a brown leather suitcase. On an ordinary Tuesday afternoon he walked into the waiting room and went straight to Gate 3 to queue. The problem was, Gate 3 had no train that day. Staff reminded him to check his ticket. He produced a paper ticket—not today's electronic printout but an old-fashioned cardboard ticket, with departure and destination stations printed in red ink and a row of small holes from a punch machine. The date on the ticket was next Tuesday. He was an entire week early. Staff told him the ticket date was for next week and asked him to return then. The man looked down at his ticket with a puzzled expression. He said: 'Impossible. My wife told me to come today. She never gets dates wrong.' Then he picked up his suitcase and left. No one noticed when he walked out of the waiting room—as if he had simply evaporated from sight. A week later on the same day, Gate 3 did have a train, but no passenger held that type of old cardboard ticket—in fact, those tickets had been discontinued fifteen years ago. The staff treated it as an odd little episode and soon forgot. Until three months later, during a routine backup of the station's security surveillance footage, a technician discovered something strange—in the Tuesday afternoon footage, the man in the Mao suit could not be seen in the waiting room. The cameras covered every corner of the waiting hall, and the recording showed that during the time period when he should have appeared, the spot where he had queued was consistently empty.
